law.text:
Chapter 4. Succession entitlement of the spouse and the legally cohabiting partner

Section 1. The surviving spouse

Art. 4.17. Inheritance rights of the surviving spouse

par. 1. When the testator leaves behind descendants, adopted children or descendants of these, the surviving spouse acquires the usufruct of the entire estate.

par. 2. When the testator leaves behind relatives in the ascending line or close collateral relatives, the surviving spouse acquires:
(1) the full ownership of the share of the first to die in the common property and in the property held exclusively in undivided ownership between the spouses, and
(2) the usufruct of the remaining goods of their own property.
(3) the usufruct of the goods subject to the right of legal reversion referred to in article 4.24 or in article 4.25, unless otherwise provided in the deed of donation or in the will.

par. 3. When the testator leaves behind other heirs or no heirs, the surviving spouse acquires the full ownership of the entire estate.

Art. 4.18. Usufruct over donated goods

Upon the death of the donor, the surviving spouse who is called to the estate receives the usufruct of the goods that the donor donated and over which the donor reserved the usufruct, on the condition that the spouse already held that capacity at the time of the donation and that the donor remained the holder of this usufruct until the day of their death.

Upon the death of the donor, the surviving spouse who is called to the estate receives the usufruct of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence at the time the estate opened and of the household effects present therein, on the condition that the donor had reserved the usufruct of those goods upon the donation of those goods, that the spouse was legally cohabiting with the donor at the time of the donation, and that the donor remained the holder of this usufruct until the day of their death.

The spouse may renounce that usufruct. Articles 4.244 to 4.253 apply to that renunciation when it takes place during the lifetime of the donor.

Art. 4.20. Right to lease

The surviving spouse acquires exclusively, to the exclusion of all other heirs, the right to the lease of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence at the time the estate of the testator opened.

Art. 4.22. Exclusion or forfeiture

par. 1. The surviving spouse may be wholly or partially excluded from or declared to have forfeited their inheritance rights if they have been wholly or partially deprived of parental authority over the children born of their marriage with the testator.

par. 2. The legal action is brought by the descendants within one year calculated either from the opening of the estate or from the deprivation of parental authority.

The judgment takes effect from the date on which the action was brought.

par. 3. If the usufruct has already been converted into the full ownership of a good or into a sum of money, or if the bare ownership of the goods referred to in article 4.61, paragraph 7, has already been transferred, the exclusion or the declaration of forfeiture gives rise to a right to compensation.

That compensation is determined by the court and corresponds to the value of the usufruct, taking into account the probable life expectancy of the usufructuary at the time the legal action was brought.

If the usufruct has been converted into a life annuity, the judgment has retroactive effect to the same point in time.

Section 2. The surviving legally cohabiting partner

Art. 4.23. Inheritance rights and right to lease of the surviving legally cohabiting partner

par. 1. The surviving legally cohabiting partner acquires, regardless of which heirs they are called to the estate with, the usufruct of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence at the time the estate of the testator opened, and of the household effects present therein.

The surviving legally cohabiting partner also exercises this right if the goods referred to in the first paragraph are the subject of a right of legal reversion referred to in article 4.24 or in article 4.25.

par. 2. The surviving legally cohabiting partner who is called to the estate receives upon the death of the donor the usufruct of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence at the time the estate opened and of the household effects present therein, if the donor had reserved the usufruct of these goods upon the donation of these goods, and on the condition that the legally cohabiting partner already held that capacity at the time of the donation and that the donor remained the holder of this usufruct until the day of their death.

The legally cohabiting partner may renounce the usufruct referred to in the first paragraph. Articles 4.244 to 4.253 apply to that renunciation when it takes place during the lifetime of the donor.

par. 3. The surviving legally cohabiting partner acquires exclusively, to the exclusion of all other heirs, the right to the lease of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence at the time the estate of the predeceased legally cohabiting partner opened, and acquires the usufruct of the household effects present therein.

par. 4. The foregoing provisions do not apply when the surviving legally cohabiting partner is a descendant of the predeceased legally cohabiting partner.

par. 5. Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary, anyone who acquires the bare ownership may demand that an inventory of the household effects and a statement of condition of the immovable property that served as the family's principal residence be drawn up.

par. 6. The rules concerning the usufruct of the surviving spouse that are included in articles 4.22 and 4.60 to 4.64 apply mutatis mutandis to the usufruct of the surviving legally cohabiting partner.

case.text: Joris and Karin were legally cohabiting. Joris dies. Karin is the surviving legal cohabitant. Karin is not a descendant of Joris. The dwelling was their principal family residence.

questions.text: Is Article 4.23, paragraph 4 a reason to deny Karin the usufruct of the family home?

expected.reason: Art. 4.23 par. 4 excludes the right only when the surviving legal cohabitant is a descendant of the predeceased cohabitant. The case states Karin is not a descendant of Joris, so that exclusion does not apply.

is_surviving_legally_cohabiting_partner_.name: is_surviving_legally_cohabiting_partner_of
is_surviving_legally_cohabiting_partner_.description: The first person is the surviving legally cohabiting partner of the second (predeceased) person.

is_descendant_of.name: is_descendant_of
is_descendant_of.description: The first person is a descendant of the second person.

foregoing_provisions_apply.name: foregoing_provisions_apply
foregoing_provisions_apply.description: The foregoing provisions apply to the surviving legally cohabiting partner (first arg) with respect to the predeceased legally cohabiting partner (second arg).

exclusion_from_foregoing_provisions_appl.name: exclusion_from_foregoing_provisions_applies
exclusion_from_foregoing_provisions_appl.description: The exclusion from the foregoing provisions applies because the surviving legally cohabiting partner (first arg) is a descendant of the predeceased legally cohabiting partner (second arg).

routing.question: Is Article 4.23, paragraph 4 a reason to deny Karin the usufruct of the family home?

deduction.label: unknown
deduction.constraint: ? x0 in Person, x1 in Person: __sel0(x0) & __sel1(x1) & exclusion_from_foregoing_provisions_applies(x0,x1)

symbolic_intent_routing.question: Is Article 4.23, paragraph 4 a reason to deny Karin the usufruct of the family home?

pipeline.natural_language: Cannot determine with certainty. exclusion_from_foregoing_provisions_applies(karin, joris) is not logically forced either way (label: unknown).
